moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with an unusual first move that prepares a flexible fianchetto setup on the kingside. The Hungarian Opening is a quiet system where we avoid committing our center pawns early, instead developing our pieces to active squares and controlling the board gradually. By playing g3, we support a future bishop fianchetto on g2, which will aim down the long diagonal toward d5 and e4—key central squares. This move is particularly clever because it invites Black to stake claims in the center, setting up what's known as a 'Reversed Alekhine' where roles are somewhat inverted from the typical Alekhine Defense structure.

Black takes the initiative and claims space in the center, which is a natural and strong response to our quiet opening. By playing e5, Black establishes a pawn on the fifth rank and opens lines for the queen and light-squared bishop, preventing us from easily executing a central thrust. We should view this as an invitation—Black has committed a pawn to e5, and once we complete our kingside fianchetto with Bg2, we'll have a nicely centralized bishop to pressure this pawn and the d5 square from a distance.

We develop our knight actively while maintaining flexibility in our position. Nf3 controls the critical central squares d4 and e5, and positions our knight where it can support multiple plans—it can reroute to e3 or d4 if needed, or stay on f3 to support a future e4 push. Notice that we're not in a hurry to play g4 immediately; instead, we develop logically and let Black decide their setup. The move also prepares castling kingside, fitting perfectly with our fianchetto scheme where our king will be tucked away safely and our bishop on g2 will help control the center.
